coredata.dc:title: Low temperature selective catalytic reduction of NOx with NH3 with improved SO2 and water resistance by using N-doped graphene dots-CuO–CeO2 nano-heterostructures modified vanadate catalysts
coredata.dc:creator: Choi, Sung Yeol
Purbia, Rahul
Kim, Hee Jun
Kim, Jin-Kyeom
Kim, Sun-Woo
Mo, Jeongeun
Ye, Bora
Jeong, Bora
Lee, Duck Hyun
Kim, Dokyoung
Park, Hyesung
Kim, Hong-Dae
Baik, Jeong Min
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we report a facile strategy for efficient NH3-SCR catalytic performance of vanadate-based catalysts with superior sulfur and water resistance at low temperature (<200 °C). The catalyst design strategy is based on the selective adsorption of NO2 via the functionalization of N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and fast-SCR via the functionalization of CuO–CeO2 (CuCe) on 4V1W/Ti catalysts. The impregnation of 1 wt% N-GQDs and 2.8 wt% CuCe leads to more surface acid sites and facilitate the strong interaction between Cu-Ce-V oxides, thus improving NOx adsorption and improved redox process in low temperature ranges (180–220 °C). The synthesized CuCe-N-GQD-4V1W/Ti catalyst shows high DeNOx activity over 92 % with excellent N2 selectivity and SO2/H2O resistance at 200 °C. In situ diffuse reflectance Fourier transform analysis confirmed the Eley–Rideal SCR reaction pathway over the CuCe-N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti catalyst.

head.ce:title: Low temperature selective catalytic reduction of NOx with NH3 with improved SO2 and water resistance by using N-doped graphene dots-CuO–CeO2 nano-heterostructures modified vanadate catalysts

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we report a facile strategy for efficient NH3-SCR catalytic performance of vanadate-based catalysts with superior sulfur and water resistance at low temperature (<200 °C). The catalyst design strategy is based on the selective adsorption of NO2 via the functionalization of N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and fast-SCR via the functionalization of CuO–CeO2 (CuCe) on 4V1W/Ti catalysts. The impregnation of 1 wt% N-GQDs and 2.8 wt% CuCe leads to more surface acid sites and facilitate the strong interaction between Cu-Ce-V oxides, thus improving NOx adsorption and improved redox process in low temperature ranges (180–220 °C). The synthesized CuCe-N-GQD-4V1W/Ti catalyst shows high DeNOx activity over 92 % with excellent N2 selectivity and SO2/H2O resistance at 200 °C. In situ diffuse reflectance Fourier transform analysis confirmed the Eley–Rideal SCR reaction pathway over the CuCe-N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti catalyst.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Selective catalytic reduction with ammonia (NH3-SCR) has been one of the effective technologies for reducing nitrogen oxide (NOX) emissions. A vanadate-based catalyst of V2O5-WO3(MoO3)/TiO2 has been commercially used at the operation temperature range of 300–400 °C in coal-fired power plants since they offer excellent denitrification ability and good resistance to sulfur [1,2]. However, despite these considerable advantages, commercially available vanadium catalyst still operates at a narrow temperature range (300–400 °C) and promote SO2 to SO3 oxidation, thus giving it a short service life and high operating costs in industrial operations [1]. To avoid deactivation (SO2, dust, alkali metals poisoning) and high-temperature cost, the SCR de-NOx reactor is placed downstream of the precipitator or the particulate control unit after the dust and sulfur removal device where the exhaust gas temperature is typically lower than 200 °C [3–6]. Therefore, this tail-end SCR reactor configuration extends the catalyst's service life and can readily be used to transform tail gas de-NOx over thermal power plants. However, commercially V2O5-WO3(MoO3)/TiO2 does not meet the requirements of lower temperature operation, as it has poor catalytic activity, deactivation (SO2 and water poisoning), and low N2 selectivity [1,7,8]. As mentioned above drawbacks of vanadium-based catalysts, it is crucial to modify vanadium catalysts with excellent de-NOx performance below 250 °C with good tolerance (SO2 and water) and N2 selectivity. In recent years, many transition metals (Ce, Mn, Cu, Fe, Co, Nb, Sn, etc.) have been used as substitutes in the existing commercial vanadium catalysts to promote the low-temperature NH3-SCR reaction [6,9–12]. In general, the substitute transition metals in vanadate affect the active V species by altering Lewis’s acid sites, oxygen mobility, and synergetic redox properties. Still, it exhibits poor activity in the presence of SO2 and H2O [13].

Aside from modifications to transition metal oxides, researchers have also used carbon modifications in the catalyst to improve the water and sulfur resistance [14]. Carbon-based catalysts have been widely studied due to their large specific surface area, low density, high mechanical strength, and their ability to regenerate and be reused. Therefore, they are highly favorable as a good supporter of vanadate-based catalysts in the SCR system. Moreover, active carbon interacts with active catalytic sites to make it easier to reduce, thus lowering the ABS (ammonium bisulfate) decomposition temperature. Carbon-based modifications such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [14–16], activated carbon (AC) [17–20], and graphene [21–23] have recently attracted substantial attention for accelerating the kinetics of the NH3-SCR reaction at low temperature. For example, the substitution of graphene into the Mn-Ti and Mn-Ce catalyst exhibits >99% NOx conversion at 180℃ and 140℃, respectively [24–26]. However, the SCR performances of these catalysts are not sufficient for commercialization because transition oxides (Ce, Mn, Cu, Fe) can easily react with SO2 to make thermally stable metal sulfates, thus weakening the redox cycle process and decreasing the catalyst performance in the presence of sulfur and water [27]. Despite this, carbon modifications to the vanadate-based catalyst can be beneficial for maintaining active sites with thermal stability and SO2 tolerance. Nevertheless, few attempts (V2O5-MoO3/TiO2-graphite [28] and mixed V2O5/rGO [29]) have been made to improve the vanadate-based catalyst SCR performance based on carbon modifications at low temperatures. However, carbon-based catalysts have been shown to suffer from the low activity and poor SO2 resistance. Also, the carbon materials were so big that the catalyst could be easily agglomerated, and active surface sites were blocked. Thus, strong interaction and good dispersion between carbon materials and vanadate catalysts are key aspects of improving low-temperature activity and SO2 resistance. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have recently attracted immense attention in several fascinating applications such as energy and catalysis because of their unusual electrical, electronic, and catalytic properties at zero-dimensional (∼1–5 nm) morphology [30,31]. To our knowledge, rare studies of GQDs-modified vanadate have been reported for SCR catalysis. In our previous study, we have shown that the N-containing basic functional groups tend to enhance the basicity of GQDs materials, which then exhibit enhanced selective NO2 adsorption at low temperatures while suppressing SO2 adsorption [32]. One appealing approach is catalyst composition modification by N-heteroatom doping in GQDs, which can modulate the adsorption energies of NOx intermediates by optimizing the electronic structure of catalysts. It is therefore expected that N-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) can be used as an emergent candidate for a cocatalyst for a vanadate-based catalyst for practically low-temperature SCR catalyst based on high NOx adsorption capability.

Inspired by our previous work [32] detailing advancements in enhanced NOx adsorption at the N-GQDs interface, we rationally design and synthesize 3-nm sized N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) to V2O5–WO3/TiO2 (4V1W/Ti) catalysts. To further improve the fast-SCR reaction, the 4V1W/Ti catalyst was modified with CuO–CeO2 (CuCe) functionalization on N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti catalysts at 180–220 °C temperature [33]. This work shows that the CuO-CeO2-N-GQDs (CuCe-N-GQDs)-modified vanadate-based catalysts exhibit excellent NOx conversion efficiencies (∼93%) at low temperatures NH3–SCR reaction (200 °C). Additionally, our optimized catalysts demonstrated outstanding resistance (93% at 54 h) toward SO2 gas compared to 4V1W/Ti catalyst (68% to 57% at 10 h). Benefiting from the advantageous features of heterostructure interface, high electronic conductivity, and enhanced adsorption induced by N-GQDs, as well as rich-redox properties and an acid cycle of CuO-CeO2, the obtained CuCe-N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti catalyst exhibits improved activity and SO2 stability in the low-temperature NH3-SCR reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and CuO-CeO2 (CuCe) composites
ce:section.ce:para: The mixed suspension of dopamine hydroxide (0.2 g) and Citric acid (0.5 g) was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and placed in a 160 °C oven for 6 h followed by filtration. For CuO-CeO2 composites [33]. ultrapure water (50 ml) was bubbled with nitrogen gas to remove dissolved oxygen. Next, 1 ml of 0.1 M cerium nitrate solution and 0.5 ml of 1 M sodium hydroxide were added to the N2 environment. Then, the solution temperature was increased by 40 °C and 1 ml of 0.1 M copper nitrate solution was mixed. After stirring for 30 min, the formed products were filtered three times by centrifugation at 10000 rpm with water and ethanol mixture (1:1). The collected nanoparticles were redispersed in 40 ml of DI water.

Firstly, 4V1W/Ti catalyst was synthesized by an incipient wetness impregnation method. First, the purchased NT-10 TiO2 powder was dispersed in 100 ml DI water. Ammonium metavanadate (0.2573 g) was dissolved in DI water (40 ml) followed by the addition of 3 ml 0.65 M oxalic acid solution to obtain an ionized vanadium solution. Similarly, ammonium metatungstate (0.1537 g) was dissolved in DI water (40 ml) to obtain an ionized tungsten solution. Then, vanadium oxalate and tungstate solution were mixed into TiO2 powder suspension. At last, the mixed catalyst suspension was dried to get the powder and heated in an atmosphere of air (550 °C for 2 h). As a reference, the obtained powder was ultimately named 4V1W/Ti as per its compositions. Then, a certain volume (2 ml) of N-GQDs (20 ml) was mixed with CuO-CeO2 and stirred overnight. After stirring, the remaining N-GQDs in the solution were removed by centrifugation. Finally, CuCe-N-GQDs were impregnated on the as-synthesized 4V1W/Ti catalyst. After incorporating the CuCe-N-GQDs on the reference catalyst, the solution was dried at 80 °C for 12 h in an oven to obtain the final CuCe-N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti catalyst. The additional experimental and catalyst characterization details were shown in the Supplementary Information
.

Our strategy to develop CuO–CeO2-N-doped graphene dots (CuCe-N-GQDs) nano-heterostructures is conceptualized on the fact that the Cu–Ce functionalization on 4V1W/Ti catalyst improves the fast SCR reaction, while the formation of heterostructure interface with N-GQDs exhibiting high electronic conductivity and enhanced NO2 adsorption [32,33]. It is well known that the fast SCR reaction (NO + 2NH3 + NO2 → 2 N2 + 3H2O) has the lowest activation barrier and the highest reaction rate at lower temperatures (<250 °C). Thus, the formation of NO2 through NO oxidation is a key aspect of the fast NH3-SCR reaction at low temperatures. In our previous work, we proposed that the redox process of Ce3+ + Cu2+ → Ce4+ + Cu+ with V4+/V5+ charge imbalance proportion stimulated NO to NO2 oxidation to enhance the low temperatures SCR activity [33]. N-GQDs were also reported to provide strong interaction and favorable adsorption with NOx molecules, even at very low temperatures below 150 °C. Benefiting from the advantageous features of the heterostructure interface, the obtained CuCe-N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti catalyst is expected to exhibit superior activity on SO2 and water resistance at low temperatures (<200 °C) NH3-SCR reaction. Fig. 1 illustrates the fabrication process of CuCe-N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti catalyst through an impregnation process. To begin, N-GQDs were synthesized under hydrothermal conditions (Fig. 1 a), which shows an average size of ∼1.2–3 nm with 0.21 nm lattice fringes in the HR-TEM (high-resolution transmission electron microscopy) image. Simultaneously, the co-precipitation method was used to synthesize CuO–CeO2 (CuCe) NPs in an inert environment (Fig. 1 b and Figure S2
,
Supplementary Information
). Typical TEM images of CuO–CeO2 nanocrystals exhibited average particle sizes of 5 (±3) nm (Fig. 1 b) with lattice spacings of 0.27 nm (100) and 0.21 nm (111) corresponding to the fluorite CeO2 and cubic CuO phase, respectively. Then, CuCe-N-GQDs composites were obtained through mixing and overnight stirring ( Figure S3
,
Supplementary Information ). V2O5-WO3-TiO2 catalysts containing 4 wt% V2O5 and 1 wt% WO3 were made by the impregnation method (named 4V1W/Ti). Optimized 1 wt% N-GQDs (1N-GQDs) and 2.8 wt% CuO–CeO2 (2.8CuCe) mixed solution were then incorporated on the 4V1W/Ti catalyst (named 2.8CuCe-1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti) as illustrated in Fig. 1 c. The FESEM image displayed uniform particles with an average size of around 30 nm (Fig. 1 d-e). In the XRD and Raman spectra (Fig. S4 a-b,
Supplementary Information ), the anatase phase was also confirmed. However, there was no peak corresponding to N-GQDs, CuO-CeO2, WO3, or V2O5 observed due to the too minimal contents to be detected or being atomically well-distributed on the support.

Further, the catalyst distribution and element local position were investigated by TEM measurements (Fig. 2 ). The TEM image of the CuCe-N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti reveals a uniform distribution of particles without agglomeration, as shown in Fig. 2 a. Fig. 2 b shows a high magnification image of CuCe-N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti, wherein CuCe-N-GQDs nanoparticles can be seen to be decorated on the 4V1W/Ti surface as per our synthesis strategy. A more magnified view (Fig. 2 c) clearly illustrated the interplanar spacing of the catalyst and additives materials. Anatase nanocrystal of around ∼26 nm in size was observed with 0.35 nm lattice spacing corresponding to the (101) plane. Other sub-5 nm size nanoparticles (V2O5, WO3) were found to be highly dispersed on the TiO2 support. The 0.21 and 0.37 nm lattice spacing were found to correspond to the (002) and (101) planes of V2O5 and WO3, respectively. A high-magnification image of the CuCe-N-GQDs evidences a different lattice fringe of the CuO–CeO2 interface (0.27 nm and 0.21 nm) and N-GQDs (0.21 nm) showing the crystalline interface. The TEM result shows that the surface of 4V1W/Ti was well decorated with CuO–CeO2-N-GQDs. Further, elemental distribution (STEM-EDS) mapping analysis also confirmed well dispersion of V, W, and CuCe-N-GQDs species throughout catalysts (Fig. 2 d-i). The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area, as well as pore size, decreased when impregnating N-GQDs and CuO-CeO2 on the catalysts, thus serving as evidence of the well-deposited co-catalyst active components on the catalyst surface. Moreover, the difference in the BET surface area indicates the modification of 4V1W/Ti catalyst with deposited N-GQDs and CuO-CeO2 species, which can alter the acid sites of the catalyst and H2-consumptions properties ( Figure S5 and Table S1,
Supplementary Information ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic activity evaluation and SO2 influence on NH3-SCR
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 3
a and Figure S6
,
Supplementary Information show NOx removal efficiencies of 4V1W/Ti with the loading of the N-GQDs (0 to 5 wt%) at 200 °C. Without N-GQDs loading, the 4V1W/Ti displayed inferior NOx conversion efficiency (<68%) at the same temperature despite the lack of SO2 gas. Such low efficiencies might have occurred due to the weak adsorbing reactive gas on active sites at low temperatures. With the loading of N-GQDs on 4V1W/Ti catalyst, the catalytic activities were enhanced up to 1 wt%. The maximum NOx conversion efficiency (91%) was obtained with 1 wt% N-GQDs loading. Further, increasing the N-GQDs content decreased NOx conversion efficiencies near the levels of reference 4V1W/Ti catalysts due to the surface coverage of active sites. Thus, 1 wt% N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti (1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti) was selected as an optimized loading for further modification process. Moreover, to maximize the utilities of the N-GQDs, CuCe mixed oxides were coated onto the catalyst, and the NOx removal efficiencies were measured as a function of various CuCe compositions (0–5 wt%) at 200 °C temperature (Fig. 3 b-c). Without CuCe loading, the 1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti exhibited 91% NOx conversion efficiencies at 200 °C temperature despite the absence of SO2 gas. With the loading of CuCe on 1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti catalyst, the NOx conversion efficiencies were significantly improved up to 2.8 wt% loading but decreased thereafter due to the blocking of catalyst active sites. Thus, 2.8CuCe-1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti was selected as a perfect candidate for further catalytic and resistance measurement study. As shown in Fig. 3 d, all catalyst samples showed over 99% N2 selectivity at 160 and 240 °C temperature ranges.

The SO2 gas in the industrial combustion exhaust is the crucial reason for the inevitable deactivation of SCR catalysts, which represents critical challenges in the SCR system, and the SO2 poisoning effect. Fig. 3 c and Figure S6
,
Supplementary Information show the NOx removal efficiencies of 4V1W/Ti, 1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti, and 2.8CuCe-1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti catalysts at various temperatures, ranging from 160 to 240 °C. The reference 4V1W/Ti demonstrated lower NOx conversion efficiencies (<68%) at 200 °C temperature despite the absence of SO2 gas. However, it increased to 88% at 240 °C temperature. When 300 ppm SO2 was introduced in the reactor, 4V1W/Ti catalysts showed a substantial reduction in NOx conversion efficiencies from 68 % to 36% at 200 °C. Such lowered NOx conversion efficiencies might be ascribed to the competitive adsorption of SO2 gas on catalytic active sites than that of NOx. With the loading of 2.8CuCe-1N-GQDs on 4V1W/Ti catalyst, the NOx removal efficiency was slightly decreased to 93 %, and there was no significant decrease in the efficiency with the introduction of SO2 gas. Thus, SO2 resistance was boosted at all tested temperatures.

To further explore the low-temperature activity and the sulfur resistance, the NOx conversion efficiencies of the optimized 2.8CuCe-1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti catalyst were measured over 52 h, compared to those of 4V1W/Ti and 1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti catalysts (Fig. 3 e). As soon as SO2 was introduced, the reference catalyst (4V1W/Ti) showed a drastic decrease in efficiency from 68 % to 57 % for 10 h. The NOx conversion efficiency of 1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti catalyst was retained at 91%, but it slowly decreased up to about 83 % up to 15 h and then decreased quickly to about 50 % at 30 h. On the other hand, there was a slight decrease in the efficiency of the 2.8CuCe-1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti catalyst for 52 h. In general, the decrease in the conversion efficiencies after long-term SO2 flow was due to the reduction in acidic sites or the formation of ammonium bisulphate (ABS) on the active sites of the catalyst [4,10,34]. To analyze the form of the sulfur species existing on the catalyst surface, thermal decomposition characteristics were determined by TGA after a long-duration SO2 reaction (52 h), as shown in Figure S7 and Table S2,
Supplementary Information . This shows that the optimized 2.8CuCe-1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti catalyst surface has an inhibiting effect on ABS formation with improved low-temperature activity. The physical appearance and chemical composition (EDX) of 4V1W/Ti and 2.8CuCe-1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti catalyst were analyzed, with the results shown in Fig. 3 f and 3g. The reference 4V1W/Ti catalysts showed the formation of the white powders on the surface, indicative as ABS, after 20 h of reaction with S element EDS mapping as deposited sulphate evidence while 2.8CuCe-1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti catalyst did not show much visual deposition of ABS on the surface with S element EDX distribution. Thus, the introduction of both CuCe oxides and N-GQDs effectively showed improved SO2 tolerance. The remarkably enhanced SO2 resistance might imply the presence of a superior redox system in 2.8CuCe-1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti catalyst which prevents the formation of sulfates and promotes the sulfates decomposition. To our knowledge, the 2.8CuCe-1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti catalyst exhibited remarkable low-temperature NOx conversion efficiency with or without the presence of SO2 compared to reference 4V1W/Ti and previously reported vanadate catalysts, as can be seen in Table S3-5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Redox properties (H2-TPR) and temperature-programmed desorption (NO, NH3)
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 a depicts the H2 consumption profiles of 4V1W/Ti, 1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti, and 2.8CuCe-1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti catalysts in a broad temperature range (100–900 °C). For the 4V1W/Ti catalyst, only a pronounced peak at 392 °C was assigned to the reduction of V5+ to V4+/3+, which is indicative of well-dispersed VOx species on the surface of the catalyst [35]. As 1 wt% N-GQDs was loaded on 4V1W/Ti catalyst, the H2 reduction peak was moved to a slightly higher at 403 °C temperature, which implied not much effect of redox cycle by N-GQDs modification. Interestingly, the reduction peak of 2.8CuCe-1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti catalyst shifted to a lower reduction temperature (353 °C), suggesting that the redox performance was improved by finely decorated Cu-Ce-V interface interaction. This lower reduction temperature can also be attributed to the easy departure of oxygen atoms from metal oxide due to Cu-Ce-V interfacial interaction, which indicates improved oxygen mobility [36,37]. This result indicated that 2.8CuCe-N-GQDs coverage could accelerate the redox cycle and the oxygen mobility of the 4V1W/Ti catalysts at lower temperatures.

The temperature-programmed desorption of NO (NO-TPD) of synthesized catalysts was displayed in Fig. 4 b. The reference 4V1W/Ti had five characteristic peaks (i.e., two peaks between 100 and 300 °C and three peaks above 400 °C). The former peaks at below 300 °C temperatures were assigned to physically adsorbed NOx species on the catalyst surface. While the latter peaks at temperatures above 400 °C were from chemically adsorbed NOx adsorbed species (nitrite and nitrate). For 1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti catalyst, the physically adsorbed NOx peaks at 221 °C became broad, and their intensity increased, indicating that N-GQDs incorporation helps to boost NO desorption at low-temperature regions. After the loading 2.8 wt% CuCe loading, the amount of physically desorbed NO was significantly enhanced compared to 4V1W/Ti and 1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti catalyst, suggesting bridged NO adsorption on the surface. It can be observed that the desorption peak area was substantially increased after the introduction of 2.8CuCe-N-GQDs, thus indicating that the presence of 2.8CuCe-N-GQDs markedly enhanced the NO adsorption of the catalyst due to the surface basicity.

The NH3-temperature-programmed desorption (NH3-TPD) was obtained to investigate the acidity, which was the crucial factor in the adsorption and activation of NH3 species. All the catalyst samples exhibited a broad desorption peak across the temperature range from 50 to 400 °C and above 400 °C, which was considered to be related to weak acid sites and strong acid sites for NH3 desorption, respectively (Fig. 4 c). For the 4V1W/Ti, three and one prominent desorption peaks were observed at the low temperature (100–400 °C) and high-temperature region (>400 °C), respectively. In the 4V1W/Ti catalyst, NH3 was intensely adsorbed on Bronsted acid sites. Compared to 4V1W/Ti, the NH3-TPD peak intensity of the 1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti catalyst dramatically broadened and increased. This indicated that the introduced 1 wt% N-GQDs species did not much affect the acid sites of the reference 4V1W/Ti catalyst. However, when 2.8 wt% Cu-Ce was loaded with N-GQDs, all desorption peak intensities were substantially enhanced, indicating enriched surface acid sites. There was also a desired temperature shift to lower temperatures, thus indicating the promotion of NH3 adsorption. The amounts of surface acid sites were estimated by integrating the NH3 desorption peak area (Fig. 4 c). It was found that the 2.8CuCe-1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti presented more acid (14.66 µmol/gm) than 4V1W/Ti (10.13 µmol /gm) and 1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti (11.14 µmol /gm). It indicated that the deposition of 2.8CuCe could improve the number of surface acid sites to adsorb NH3, and then facilitate the activation and adsorption of NH3, thus enhancing the catalytic activity as demonstrated in our previous study [33].

To check the chemical stability after the SO2 resistance test, the H2-TPR and surface adsorption (NO-TPD and NH3-TPD) of 4V1W/Ti, 1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti, and 2.8CuCe-1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti catalyst have also been measured after the SO2 resistance test and compared with those of the fresh catalyst. The 2.8CuCe-1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti samples indicate no severe deterioration of catalytic reducibility upon SO2 poisoning at 200 °C (Fig. 4 a). As shown in Fig. 4 a, the used 4V1W/Ti, 1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti, and 2.8CuCe-1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti catalyst samples (after SO2 stability) exhibited an additionally strong peak at a range from 550 to 700 °C due to the reduction of surface sulfate species in the form of H2S [38–40]. Further, no serious deterioration of NH3, and NO adsorption was noticed after long-duration SO2 resistance at a low-temperature range for the 2.8CuCe-N-GQD-4V1W/Ti catalyst ( Fig. 4 b-c), indicating better physicochemical stability. However, in the case of NO-TPD of used 4V1W/Ti catalyst, the NO desorption intensity dropped a lot in the 400–800 °C range compared to fresh 4V1W/Ti catalyst. After SO2 resistance test, the presence of sulfate deposition on the surface of 4V1W/Ti catalyst hindered the NO adsorption at high temperatures, indicating sulfate occupied the corresponding active sites of the catalyst.

XPS spectroscopy (X-ray photoelectron) was conducted to know the composition and the chemical valence state of the catalysts. In the V 2p core level spectra of 4V1W/Ti catalysts, V 2p 3/2 and V 2p 1/2 of V5+ were respectively observed at binding energies of around 517.80 and 524.1 eV (Fig. 5
a). In addition, the corresponding binding energies for V2p 3/2 and V 2p 1/2 of V4+ were detected at around 516.9 and 522.9 eV, respectively. The calculated V4+/(V4++V5+) ratio was found to be about 0.55 for 4V1W/Ti and increased to 0.63 and 0.83 for 1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti and 2.8CuCe-1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti, respectively. Previous reports have revealed that lower valency (V4+) of vanadium oxides can significantly generate active oxygen species, which can boost the redox cycles (V4+/V5+) to improve the charge imbalance properties during SCR reaction [2,7,14,41,42]. The binding energy difference between V 2p 3/2 and V 2p 1/2 was increased (Table S6) when CuCe was incorporated, thus suggesting an effective electronic interaction between V and CuCe species [43]. In the SCR reaction, active oxygen can participate in both adsorbed gas phase and lattice oxygen reactions. With increasing in temperature, the predominant chemisorbed oxygen species changes from molecular O2 to atomic O-/O2 – ones above 200 °C temperature, which is helpful for the formation of chemisorbed oxygen on the catalyst surface. The increase in chemisorbed oxygen creates the charge imbalance and oxygen vacancy, facilitating the adsorption of NOx and NH3 to ensure a better denitrification efficiency of the catalyst at 200 °C temperature. As presented in Fig. 5 b, the XPS spectra of O 1 s exhibited two characteristic peaks at around 528.9 eV and 531.1 eV attributed to lattice oxygen (Olatt) species and surface adsorbed oxygen (Oads) species, respectively. Interestingly, the relative intensities of the peaks corresponding to the Oads were increased with the modification with N-GQDs (0.60) and CuCe-N-GQDs (0.65), indicating the existence of oxygen vacancies compared to 4V1W/Ti catalyst (0.23). Table S7 summarizes the atomic concentrations and ratio of surface adsorbed oxygen (Oads) to lattice oxygen (Olatt) species based on XPS survey spectra. Simultaneously, Olatt to Oads and V4+/(V4++V5+) ratio was affected after the Cu-Ce incorporation in the 4V1W/Ti catalyst indicating induction of the charge imbalance through excellent redox system (strong interaction of Cu, Ce, and V oxides), as well as the increased surface absorbed oxygen as vacancies. In addition, the appearance of XPS peaks between 518 and 521.8 eV is attributed to the X-ray satellite of the O1s core level.

The charge imbalance is also evident in the Ce 3d core level spectra ( Figure S11
,
Supplementary Information ), wherein three pairs of spin–orbit doublets—including six deconvoluted peaks were observed. Two major regions at 881–890 and 898–908 eV respectively corresponded to Ce 3d 5/2 and Ce 3d 3/2. Typically, peaks marked as u′ (903.63 eV) and v′ (885.7 eV) represent Ce3+ whereas peaks marked as u (900.64 eV), u′′ (905.87 eV), v (882.2 eV), and v′′ (887.80 eV) represent Ce4+. The co-existence of Ce4+ to Ce3+ could initiate more oxygen vacancies and redox process through charge imbalance, which promotes the adsorption of oxygen species and activates surface reactants in the SCR process.

After SO2 tolerance test at 200 °C, the XPS spectra of V 2p and S 2p were further analyzed. As shown in Fig. 5 c, the V4+/(V4++V5+) ratio of 2.8CuCe-1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti was more highly maintained compared to those of 4V1W/Ti and 1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti catalysts. This means that the lower valence states of vanadium oxides are contributed to the catalytic reactions through V-Ce-Cu elements redox interaction and charge imbalance, promoting the low-temperature NH3-SCR activity. Moreover, the V5+ proportion was increased after the reaction, which indicates a charge imbalance of V5+/4+ species. As shown in Fig. 5 d, the XPS spectra of 4V1W/Ti and 1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti displayed two S 2p peaks attributed to SO3 2− (∼168.2 eV) and SO4 2− (∼169.8 eV). These results prove that the oxidization state of SO2 tends to exist on the surface of 4V1W/Ti and 1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti. Despite this, the existence of SO2 was conquered in the 2.8CuCe-1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti catalyst. Overall, the improved SO2-resistant could be ascribed to better redox Cu-Ce-V interaction, and surface acid properties corresponding to optimized redox activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In situ DRIFTS, NO to NO2 conversion and resistance test (SO2 & H2O)
ce:section.ce:para: To clarify the adsorption and the reaction intermediates formed over the 4V1W/Ti, 1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti and 2.8CuCe-1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti catalysts in the NH3-SCR process, in-situ diffuse reflectance infrared Fourier transform spectroscopy (DRIFTS) measurements were conducted, and the results are presented in Fig. 6 . After being exposed to NO + O2, the surface of the 4V1W/Ti catalyst was covered by several NOx species [44,45]. In Fig. 6 a, the bands at 1240 and 1650 cm−1 are assigned to bridging nitrate, whereas the bands at 1282 cm−1 are attributed to monodentate nitrates. The band at 1560 cm−1 is assigned to bidentate nitrates, while the band at 1445 cm−1 is attributed to nitrite species [2]. However, after N-GQDs modification, the 1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti catalysts exhibited enhanced NOx species compared to the reference 4V1W/Ti catalyst and suggesting the existence of improved bridged NO adsorption on the surface of N-GQDs. In Fig. 6 a, the intensity of bridging nitrate bands at 1100–1240 and 1650 cm−1 are increased. Also, the bands at 1560 cm−1 and 1445 cm−1 corresponding to bidentate nitrates, and nitrite species also enhanced. We can conclude that the amount of physically adsorbed NO was significantly increased in 1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti catalysts compared to that 4V1W/Ti catalyst, suggesting the existence of bridged NO adsorption on the surface. The increased physically adsorbed NO was also evident from NO-TPD and gas-sensing experiments. The 2.8CuCe-1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti catalysts exhibited similar NOx species as the reference 4V1W/Ti and 1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti catalysts; however, the NO2 (1620 cm−1) and bridged nitrate (1560 cm−1) species intensity increased with exposure time (Fig. 6 a). The amount of bridged nitrate species and physically adsorbed nitrate was significantly increased in 2.8CuCe-1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti catalysts, suggesting the existence of bridged nitrate species (NO2) and physically adsorbed nitrate (NO) could induce fast SCR, which exhibited better activity at low temperature [3].

In reference 4V1W/Ti catalyst, the band at 1463 cm−1 belonging to the adsorption of NH3 on Brønsted acid is not detected over 20 min; however, on Brønsted acid sites, it appeared after 30 min NH3 injection (Fig. 6 b). The peak intensity of the NH4 + species on Brønsted acid on the 4V1W/Ti catalyst is much more intense than those of Lewis acid sites peaks after 30 min. After modification with N-GQDs, the 1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti catalyst exhibits improved Lewis’s acid sites on the surface from the initial reaction time to 30 min reaction. On the other hand, the peak intensity of the NH4 + species on Brønsted acid also becomes much more intense with an NH3 exposure time of up to 30 min reaction. It can be deduced that the peak intensity of Brønsted acid sites and Lewis’s acid improved and coexist with reaction time after modification with N-GQDs. For 2.8CuCe-N-GQDS-4V1W/Ti catalyst, the adsorbed NH3 species on Brønsted acid sites and Lewis’s acid sites can be equally observed after 10 min of NH3 injection time. The peak intensity of Brønsted acid sites and Lewis’s acid increased with reaction time; as a result, Brønsted acid sites and Lewis’s acid sites coexist on the CuCe-N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti catalyst surface. The high relative ratio of the Brønsted acid site peaks (1463(B)/1589(L)) was found in the 2.8CuCe-N-GQDS-4V1W/Ti catalyst surface, revealing that the addition of CuCe-N-GQDs could also increase Brønsted acid sites at low temperature [46–48]. The DRIFTS experimental results suggest that the addition of CuCe-N-GQDS increases surface acid sites and strengthens their stability and promotes the formation of various intermediate NO bridging species (nitrite and nitrate) on the surface. This suggests that the addition of CuCe-N-GQDS to a 4V1W/Ti catalyst could produce more coordinated NH3 and ionic NH4 + species to enhance the low-temperature activity. Thus, the adsorbed NH3 on both Lewis and Brønsted acid sites and intermediate bridging nitrate species are the major characteristics in the transient fast SCR reaction, following the Eley − Rideal (E − R) mechanism as per literature [1,11,49–53].

Many studies have shown that NO2 is a key molecule for improving low-temperature SCR activity via the fast-SCR reaction [1,11]. We also observed an enhanced NO to NO2 conversion rate in 2.8CuCe-1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti catalyst under SO2 gas (300 ppm) with temperature, which ascribed to increased surface acid sites via CuCe modification ( Figure S8
a,
Supplementary Information ). The enhanced NO to NO2 conversion rate in 2.8CuCe-1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti catalyst ascribed to synergistic effects of N-GQDs and CuCe modification. The N-GQDs addition improved bridged NO adsorption as well as improved Lewis’s acid sites on the surface as also evidenced by TPD and gas-sensing analysis. While the CuCe modification increased bridged nitrate species (NO2) to physically adsorbed nitrate (NO) ratio to induce fast SCR with a high relative ratio of the Brønsted acid site peaks [54]. Further, the adsorption of NO2 and SO2 gas on the surface of N-GQDs was evaluated by fabricating resistive type sensors consisting of pristine TiO2 and 1 wt% N-GQDs decorated TiO2, respectively ( Figures S8
a and S8b,
Supplementary Information ). It can be concluded that CuO-CeO2 materials can act as NO activation sites and that partially covered N-GQDs on CuO-CeO2 have the ability to make gas molecules selectively adsorbed. Finally, to elaborate on the SO2 & H2O resistance of the catalysts, the catalytic performance was evaluated under 10 vol% H2O and 300 ppm SO2, as indicated in Figure S9
b,
Supplementary Information . After introducing H2O into the SO2 atmosphere, the NOx conversion decreased from 69 % to 52 % and from 91 % to 84 % for 4V1W/Ti and 1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti catalysts at 200 °C, respectively. Meanwhile, the NOx conversion of 2.8CuCe-1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti catalyst a little decreased from 93 % to 92 %. The decrease in the NOx conversion efficiency of 4V1W/Ti and 1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti catalyst mainly results from the ABS deposition on the active sites and competitive adsorption between NO and H2O [34,55,56]. To analyze the longer period sulfur and water resistance experiments, the NOx conversion efficiency of catalyst was further investigated at 200 °C for 350 min in the presence of SO2 (300 ppm) and H2O (10 vol%) as shown in Figure S9
(c),
Supplementary Information . The NO conversion of 4V1W/Ti and 1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti catalysts showed a gradual decrease after the feeding of SO2 and H2O. While the NO conversion of the 2.8CuCe-1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti catalysts slightly decreased to 82% after 350 min but was still much higher than that on 4V1W/Ti and 1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti. These results suggest that the 2.8CuCe-1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti catalyst performed superior DeNOx efficiency than 1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti and 4V1W/Ti catalyst under sulfur and water resistance experiments. However, in the co-existence of SO2/H2O, 2.8CuCe-1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti catalyst exhibited excellent performance and may reduce the sulfation of surface active sites and the ABS formation due to better CuCe redox ability and SO2 resistance [33,57–59]. Long-term operation and physicochemical stability are important prerequisites for a catalyst in real-life applications. Based on our long-term operation (with SO2 and co-existence of SO2/H2O) and physicochemical stability (before and after catalytic testing), we implicitly conclude that our 2.8CuCe-1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti catalyst does not much affect the catalytic properties as well as reducibility and desorption properties at a low-temperature range compared to N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti and 4V1W/Ti catalyst.

According to prior studies and the analyses of the results, a possible catalytic mechanism is proposed. The loading of CuCe improved the surface acidic properties and V5+/V4+ redox ability through the dual redox cycle (Ce3+ + Cu2+ → Ce4+ + Cu+ and Ce4+ + V4+↔ Ce3+ + V5+). The Cu-Ce-V species can cause the charge imbalance to form oxygen vacancies, thus resulting in the adsorption of gaseous oxygen. Therefore, more chemisorbed oxygen is formed on the catalyst surface, which can facilitate the oxidization of NO to NO2. The N-GQDs species synergistically improved NO2 adsorption on the catalyst surface. More adsorbed NO2 easily reacted with the adsorbed NH3 to generate H2O and N2 as a fast SCR reaction. Moreover, the abundant acid sites on the surface of the 2.8CuCe-1N-GQDs-4V1W/Ti could simultaneously supply more sites for NH3 adsorption. Overall, the deposition of a small amount of CuCe-N-GQDs to the catalyst produced a combined effect of the enriched surface acidity, charge imbalance, oxygen vacancies, enhanced NO2 adsorption, and the dual Cu-Ce-V oxides redox system, favoring better NO oxidation at low temperature. The remarkable SO2 resistance could also be endorsed by the facile electron transfer in the Cu-Ce-V redox cycle to prevent the electron transfer from SO2 to Ce4+ or V4+, thus conquering the sulfate species formation on the surface. Moreover, the N-GQDs species synergistically suppressed the SO2 adsorption on the surface of the catalysts. This work provides a facile approach for adding SO2&H2O resistance to vanadate catalyst by coating CuCe-N-GQDs, which have unique selective adsorption and redox properties at low temperatures.

In summary, we reported the superior DeNOx performance of N-doped graphene quantum dots-CuO-CeO2 nano-heterostructures coated on vanadate-based catalysts synthesized through an impregnation process. The results of microstructural analysis using SEM and TEM tools revealed that CuCe and N-GQDs NPs (<4nm) were well distributed on 4V1W/Ti catalyst surface. At 200 °C, the impregnation of 1 wt% N-GQDs and 2.8 wt% CuO-CeO2 showed quite high NOx conversion efficiencies (∼91 %) under SO2 (300 ppm) and water (10 vol%) environments, along with excellent long-term stability up to 52 hrs under SO2. The remarkable low-temperature activity originated from the high NO to NO2 conversion rate (>20 % at 200 °C), and it selectively enhanced the adsorption of NO2 gas, compared to SO2 gas, and also enhanced the surface acidic properties. In H2-TPR results, the reduction peaks were shifted to lower temperatures (180–220 °C), suggesting enhanced redox ability and remarkable reducing properties of catalyst at low temperatures. In addition, the NO-TPD and NH3-TPD analysis also evidenced that the impregnation of 1 wt% N-GQDs and 2.8 wt% CuCe generated additional surface acid sites to promote the NO and NH3 adsorption/activation at low temperatures. Interestingly, the XPS results showed that atomically dispersed CuCe-N-GQDs species created oxygen vacancies, charge imbalance, and an excellent redox ability (Cu, Ce, and V oxides interaction) that are known to be advantageous properties for boosting NH3-SCR activity at low temperatures. The results of in situ DRIFTS studies revealed that the E–R route between gaseous NO and adsorbed NH3 was dominant at the catalytic reaction of CuCe-N-GQDs nano-heterostructure catalysts. This work provides a facile approach for adding SO2&H2O resistance to vanadate catalyst by coating CuCe-N-GQDs, which have unique selective adsorption and redox properties at low temperatures.
